items.md: In *Der Führer's Face* (1943), the pressure of laboring on an assembly line drives Donald mad, but this experience is doubly displaced because he is working in a Nazi war plant, not an American assembly line, and because it turns out to be a nightmare. *Donald's Gold Mine* (1942) also features a nightmarish assembly line sequence that is displaced by being located in a mine, not an industrial plant. Barks worked on neither film.
items.value: In Der Führer's Face (1943), the pressure of laboring on an assembly line drives Donald mad, but this experience is doubly displaced because he is working in a Nazi war plant, not an American assembly line, and because it turns out to be a nightmare. Donald's Gold Mine (1942) also features a nightmarish assembly line sequence that is displaced by being located in a mine, not an industrial plant. Barks worked on neither film.

items.md: When Barks reworked this plot for the comics (*WDC&S* #100 [June 1949]), Donald became the one to triumph, opening the schoolhouse and making the boys write "Crime Does Not Pay" on the blackboard. In books that children could read and reread, open rebellion was not as permissible as in a film that flashed by quickly on the screen.
items.value: When Barks reworked this plot for the comics (WDC&S #100 [June 1949]), Donald became the one to triumph, opening the schoolhouse and making the boys write "Crime Does Not Pay" on the blackboard. In books that children could read and reread, open rebellion was not as permissible as in a film that flashed by quickly on the screen.
See Sea Scouts (1939) and The Vanishing Private (1942).

items.md: Barks's action scenes often culminate in a tableau gag, a held picture showing Donald in a ridiculous pose. In *The Hockey Champ*, he receives horns and a pitchfork of ice that make him look satanic; in *Snow Fight* he gets frozen atop a plume of ice, looking like a monkey on a palm tree.

When Barks reworked this plot for the comics (*WDC&S* #100 [June 1949]), Donald became the one to triumph, opening the schoolhouse and making the boys write "Crime Does Not Pay" on the blackboard. In books that children could read and reread, open rebellion was not as permissible as in a film that flashed by quickly on the screen.

64. Barks's action scenes often culminate in a tableau gag, a held picture showing Donald in a ridiculous pose. In *The Hockey Champ*, he receives horns and a pitchfork of ice that make him look satanic; in *Snow Fight* he gets frozen atop a plume of ice, looking like a monkey on a palm tree.

items.md: Al Taliaferro had created the dog in the Donald Duck newspaper strip and had named him Bolivar. Barks had to change the name to Bornworthy because Western Publishing thought that Latin Americans might be offended because the name defamed their national hero, Simon Bolivar.

items.md: Deconstruction is "a mode of reading texts which subverts the implicit claim of a text to possess adequate grounds, in the system of language it deploys, to establish its own structure, unity and determinate meanings" (M. H. Abrams, *A Glossary of Literary Terms*, 4th ed. [New York: Holt, Rinehart, and Winston, 1981], 38).
items.value: Deconstruction is "a mode of reading texts which subverts the implicit claim of a text to possess adequate grounds, in the system of language it deploys, to establish its own structure, unity and determinate meanings" (M. H. Abrams, A Glossary of Literary Terms, 4th ed. [New York: Holt, Rinehart, and Winston, 1981], 38).

items.md: Bugs Bunny was the first funny-animal superhero in comic books. "Super-Dooper Rabbit" appeared in *Looney Tunes* #5 (March 1942). Dana Coty, who gave Barks the idea for a superhero parody, was probably inspired by this story. The name "Super Snooper" came from *Funnyman* #6 (1948), a comic book starring a comedian superhero created by Superman creators Jerry Siegel and Joe Shuster.
items.value: Bugs Bunny was the first funny-animal superhero in comic books. "Super-Dooper Rabbit" appeared in Looney Tunes #5 (March 1942). The name "Super Snooper" came from Funnyman #6 (1948), a comic book starring a comedian superhero created by Superman creators Jerry Siegel and Joe Shuster.
